```{r python-setup-new-reticulate}
#| include: false
#| eval: true
library(reticulate)
# New way to say which packages/libraries we want access to
py_require(packages = c("pandas, matplotlib"))

# This creates an ephemeral environment once per R session and loads libraries
# Would be faster to manually create an environment, install libraries to it
# then point to it with the use_python() or use_condaenv etc.. commands
# however then would need to edit it if you work on different machines

```

# Chapter 4 - Nicer boxes and Python

This chapter shows off some nice coloured boxes. They come from the `custom-numbered-blocks` extension. First you need to install the extension `quarto add ute/custom-numbered-blocks`.

This code has then been added somewhere (`_quarto.yml` or `_metadata.yml` or at the top of this file) to load the coloured boxes. In reality it's link to a file called `_numbered-boxes.yml` from in `_quarto.yml`. It looks approximately like this, you define groups of like-numbered boxes, and then define specific classes (and their names later). They can have separate colour schemes too.

```
filters:
  - custom-numbered-blocks # This loads the extension

# This is ute-hahn's nice coloured boxes: setup
custom-numbered-blocks:
  groups:
    thmlike:
      colors: [948bde, 584eab]
      boxstyle: foldbox.simple
    deflike:
      colors: [ffdc36, ffb948]
      boxstyle: foldbox.simple
    videolike:
      colors: [beb9eb, 877eda]
      boxstyle: foldbox.simple
      collapse: open
  classes:
    Theorem:
      group: thmlike
    Lemma:
      group: thmlike
    Corollary:
      group: thmlike
    Definition:
      group: deflike
    Video:
      group: videolike
      
crossref:
  chapters: true
```

## Custom Numbered Blocks

We’ve defined several custom-numbered block types using the `custom-numbered-blocks` option. The key point is that these can be numbered and follow grouped numbering conventions.

Each block belongs to a **group** that determines its shared numbering. 

All of these blocks are *foldable*, meaning they can be opened and closed. The `collapse` setting determines the starting state.

<style>
.colorbox {
  display: inline-block;
  width: 1em;
  height: 1em;
  border: 1px solid #ccc;
  border-radius: 2px;
  margin-right: 0.3em;
  vertical-align: middle;
}
</style>

| Box Name | Group | Title Background Color | Left Border Color | Default State |
|-----------|--------|------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Theorem   | `thmlike`  | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#948bde;"></span>`#948bde` | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#584eab;"></span>`#584eab` | Open / Closeable |
| Lemma     | `thmlike`  | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#b2dac4;"></span>`#b2dac4` | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#004721;"></span>`#004721` | Open / Closeable |
| Corollary | `thmlike`  | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#7fbad7;"></span>`#7fbad7` | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#005398;"></span>`#005398` | Open / Closeable |
| Definition| `deflike`   | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#ffdc36;"></span>`#ffdc36` | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#ffb948;"></span>`#ffb948` | Open / Closeable |
| Video     | `videolike` | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#d18888;"></span>`#d18888` | <span class="colorbox" style="background-color:#660000;"></span>`#660000` | Open / Closeable |

: This is the table's caption {#tbl-boxcols tbl-colwidths="[15,20,25,20,20]"}

These boxes also have dark-mode versions, see below. Tables like this can have captions, and be referenced @tbl-boxcols. They can also have styling added.

> Note:
> The two colors defined for each group represent the **left border** and the **title background**.

Here's an alternative to treating videos are float/figures, put them in their own box and use the title of the box to describe the video.

:::{.Video #vidFirst}
## Binomial response models applied to beetle mortality data (9m59)  

There is some text here before the video

{{< video https://youtu.be/d2nG7Y17sQw 
  title = "Binomial response models applied to beetle mortality data (9m59s)"
>}}
:::

:::{.example}
### Beetle mortality data

Here's some famous beetle data. The R code is folded (and thus hideable), the Python code isn't folded so cannot be folded away.

::::::{.panel-tabset}

### R

```{r r-beetle-load}
#| code-fold: show
beetles <- read.csv(url("http://www.stats.gla.ac.uk/~tereza/rp/beetles.csv"))
beetles
```

### Python

```{python py-beetle-load, eval=TRUE}
import pandas as pd
beetles = pd.read_csv("../resources/data/beetles.csv")
beetles
```

::::::

:::

:::{.callout-tip}
## Conventional tip callout

Some content
:::

:::{.Lemma #bestlemma}
## Super

This is a fantastic Lemma.
:::

There are no Lemma's better than Lemma \ref{bestlemma}. Note that referencing of these coloured boxes works like LaTeX's `\ref{}` command, thus it just renders the number, and you have to write the object name in front. This is in contrast to the `@lem-name` Quarto notation.

The other way to embed a video is inside a fenced div and treat it like a figure, as we saw in other chapters.

Our video in this chapter was embedded inside a custom numbered block and it was called [Video \ref{vidFirst}](#vidFirst).

## Demonstration of Custom Numbered Blocks

Below are examples of the custom-numbered blocks.  
Each block is foldable (open or closed by default) and has its own colour scheme and group style as defined in your YAML configuration.

Notice Theorem, Corollary and Lemma has been defined to share counter. Unlike Video and Definition have their own types (*deflike* and *vidlike*).

::: {.Theorem #rthmpythag}
## Pythagoras' Theorem

In a right-angled triangle...
:::

::: {.Corollary #Rcor-pythag}
## Corollary to Pythagoras' Theorem

In a right-angled triangle, if the two shorted sides are equal, so that $a = b$, then the hypotenuse satisfies $c=a\sqrt{2}$.
:::

::: {.Lemma #Rlem-euclid}
## Euclid

If two lines are perpendicular to the same line, then they are parallel to each other.
:::

::: {.Video #Rvid-yt}
## Video Demonstration
 
Here's a Youtube video:

{{< video https://youtu.be/d2nG7Y17sQw 
  title = "Binomial response models applied to beetle mortality data (9m59s)"
>}}
:::

::: {.Definition #Rdef-orthog} 
## Definition of Orthogonality

Two vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are said to be *orthogonal* if their dot product is zero:
$$
\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = 0.
$$
:::

---

## Referencing the Blocks

You can reference the blocks defined above using their labels:

- The main result is stated in Theorem \ref{rthmpythag}. 
- Checkout Corollary \ref{Rcor-pythag}.  
- The proof uses Lemma \ref{Rlem-euclid}.  
- Then there's Definition \ref{Rdef-orthog}.  
- Finally, see Video \ref{Rvid-yt}.

If we want we can wrap the `\ref{}` inside a hyperlink to get [Theorem \ref{rthmpythag}](#rthmpythag), [Corollary \ref{Rcor-pythag}](#Rcor-pythag), etc...

## Customization

Each box has to define four colours. In the file mentioned above the light-mode border and background are set. For those using dark-mode you also need to define a dark mode pair. See [dark styles boxes stylesheet](../themes/dark-styles-boxes.scss).

## More Python

Rendering documents containing R and Python is based on the `reticulate` and `knitr` R packages.

One way is to let quarto create you a python environment to install your packages into via `quarto install python`.

If you aleady have python installed then there's the hard way or the easy way.

### The easy way

You just run this:

```{{r python-setup-new-reticulate}}
#| include: false
#| eval: true

library(reticulate)

# New way to say which packages/libraries we want access to
py_require(packages = c("pandas, matplotlib"))
```

This creates an ephemeral environment once per R session and installs libraries for you, ready for them to be imported later. The **slight drawback** I see is that once per session (the first time you compile) your computer will take some time to go and install the libraries for you into it's ephemeral environment. The **big advantage** is it doesn't matter what machine you're on, you don't need to point your file to where to find python/anaconda/miniconda/envs. You merely need to know in advance which libraries will be used.

### The harder manual way

For the manual approach you can use *virtual enviroments* or Anaconda/Conda like this:

1. Create an [ana]conda environment for this project like this: (and install your libraries/packages into it)
```
conda create -n quarto python=3.13
conda activate quarto
conda install numpy matplotlib
```

2. Set your Python environment for this document near the top:

```{r}
#| label: python-setup-fake
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

library(reticulate)
reticulate::use_condaenv(condaenv = "quarto", conda = "auto", required = TRUE)

#to check it's loaded
#py_config() 
```

Do something, with separate tabs for R and Python:

::: {.panel-tabset}

## R

```{r}
#| label: fig-rnorm
#| fig-cap: "A base R plot"
#| fig-alt: "Alt text"

x <- rnorm(50)
plot(x)
```

## Python

```{python eval=TRUE}
#| label: fig-polar
#| fig-cap: "A line plot on a polar axis"
#| fig-alt: "Alt text"

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

r = np.arange(0, 2, 0.01)
theta = 2 * np.pi * r
fig, ax = plt.subplots(
  subplot_kw = {'projection': 'polar'} 
)
ax.plot(theta, r)
ax.set_rticks([0.5, 1, 1.5, 2])
ax.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

:::

The output images from these pieces of code have their own labels namely `@fig-rnorm` and `@fig-polar` which reference as @fig-rnorm and @fig-polar.
